{{Taxobox | color = lightgreen | name = Nokishinobu (Lepisorus thunbergianus) | image = [[File:Lepisorus thunbergiana nokisinobu.jpg|250px|''Lepisorus thunbergiana'']] | image_caption = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' in Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | status = | regnum = Plantae | divisio = Pteridophyta | classis = Polypodiopsida | ordo = Polypodiales | familia = Polypodiaceae | subfamilia = Microsoroideae | genus = Lepisorus | species = '''''L. thunbergianus''''' | binomial = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' | binomial_authority = (Kaulf.) Ching }}
'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}

== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.

'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}

'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sn:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[sn:species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5876449]] Wikidata name: Lepisorus thunbergianus [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Taxobox | color = lightgreen | name = Nokishinobu (Lepisorus thunbergianus) | image = [[File:Lepisorus thunbergiana nokisinobu.jpg|250px|''Lepisorus thunbergiana'']] | image_caption = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' in Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | status = | regnum = Plantae | divisio = Pteridophyta | classis = Polypodiopsida | ordo = Polypodiales | familia = Polypodiaceae | subfamilia = Microsoroideae | genus = Lepisorus | species = '''''L. thunbergianus''''' | binomial = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' | binomial_authority = (Kaulf.) Ching }}
'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}
== External Links == * {{NCBI|32163|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (NCBI) * {{EOL|487138|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (Encyclopedia of Life) * [https://www1.ous.ac.jp/garden/hada/plantsdic/pteridophyta/polypodiaceae/nokisinobu/nokisinobu.htm 岡山理科大学 - ノキシノブ 植物図鑑]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sn:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[sn:species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5876449]] Wikidata name: Lepisorus thunbergianus [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Taxobox | color = lightgreen | name = Nokishinobu (Lepisorus thunbergianus) | image = [[File:Lepisorus thunbergiana nokisinobu.jpg|250px|''Lepisorus thunbergiana'']] | image_caption = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' in Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | status = | regnum = Plantae | divisio = Pteridophyta | classis = Polypodiopsida | ordo = Polypodiales | familia = Polypodiaceae | subfamilia = Microsoroideae | genus = Lepisorus | species = '''''L. thunbergianus''''' | binomial = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' | binomial_authority = (Kaulf.) Ching }}
'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}
== External Links == * {{NCBI|32163|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (NCBI) * {{EOL|487138|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (Encyclopedia of Life) * [https://www1.ous.ac.jp/garden/hada/plantsdic/pteridophyta/polypodiaceae/nokisinobu/nokisinobu.htm 岡山理科大学 - ノキシノブ 植物図鑑]

Ching }}
'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[co:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[co:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sn:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[sn:species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]]
{{Taxobox | color = lightgreen | name = Nokishinobu (Lepisorus thunbergianus) | image = [[File:Lepisorus thunbergiana nokisinobu.jpg|250px|''Lepisorus thunbergiana'']] | image_caption = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' in Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | status = | regnum = Plantae | divisio = Pteridophyta | classis = Polypodiopsida | ordo = Polypodiales | familia = Polypodiaceae | subfamilia = Microsoroideae | genus = Lepisorus | species = '''''L. thunbergianus''''' | binomial = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' | binomial_authority = (Kaulf.) Ching }}
'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}
== External Links == * {{NCBI|32163|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (NCBI) * {{EOL|487138|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (Encyclopedia of Life) * [https://www1.ous.ac.jp/garden/hada/plantsdic/pteridophyta/polypodiaceae/nokisinobu/nokisinobu.htm 岡山理科大学 - ノキシノブ 植物図鑑]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[co:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[co:commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[ja:ノキシノブ]][[ko:일엽초]][[Category:sn_crud|species:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[sv:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[vi:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[war:Lepisorus thunbergianus]][[zh:瓦韦]]
{{Taxobox | color = lightgreen | name = Nokishinobu (Lepisorus thunbergianus) | image = [[File:Lepisorus thunbergiana nokisinobu.jpg|250px|''Lepisorus thunbergiana'']] | image_caption = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' in Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | status = | regnum = Plantae | divisio = Pteridophyta | classis = Polypodiopsida | ordo = Polypodiales | familia = Polypodiaceae | subfamilia = Microsoroideae | genus = Lepisorus | species = '''''L. thunbergianus''''' | binomial = ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' | binomial_authority = (Kaulf.) Ching }}
'''Nokishinobu''' (''Lepisorus thunbergianus''), commonly referred to as the '''weeping fern''' in English, is a species of epiphytic fern belonging to the family Polypodiaceae. The plant's Japanese name derives from its growth habit, often found hanging under eaves (''nokishita'') like the fern species ''shinobu.''
== Description == ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' features short, creeping rhizomes covered with numerous scales. It attaches itself to tree bark or rocky surfaces, utilizing its slender roots for anchorage.
The leaves are narrow, simple, and somewhat thickened, resembling willow leaves. The leaves are linear to lanceolate, tapering to a pointed tip. They exhibit a glossy, yellow-green surface and curl inward when dry. The underside hosts rows of rounded spore sacs (sori) arranged symmetrically along the midrib.
=== Spore Production === The sori are spherical and protrude slightly from the leaf's surface. These structures facilitate reproduction via spores, which disperse readily in the wind.
<gallery> File:Starr 041221-1869 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Foliage File:Starr 030222-0105 Lepisorus thunbergianus.jpg|Underside with sori </gallery>
== Distribution == This species is native to temperate and tropical regions of East Asia, including: - Japan (south of Hokkaido) - Korea (southern regions) - China - Indochina - The Philippines
In Japan, it thrives in lowland forests, growing on trees, rocks, or man-made structures such as garden lanterns and temple walls.
== Relationship with Humans == While ''Lepisorus thunbergianus'' is not actively cultivated, its presence on trees or stone walls is often regarded as aesthetically pleasing. Rare variations with serrated leaves are prized as ornamental plants in the niche field of wild mountain flora cultivation.
== Taxonomy and Related Species == The genus ''Lepisorus'' comprises approximately 50 species distributed across tropical and temperate Asia. Among them, Japan is home to around 10 species, many resembling ''L. thunbergianus'' in appearance.
=== Selected Species in Japan: === * '''''Lepisorus annuifrons''''' (Hotai Fern) * '''''Lepisorus onoei''''' (Himenokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus tosaensis''''' (Tsukushinokishinobu) * '''''Lepisorus uchiyamae''''' (Kouraboshi)
Taxonomic differentiation among species often requires detailed examination of scales on the rhizomes and spore arrangements.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor = 岩槻邦男編 |title=日本の野生植物 シダ |publisher=平凡社 |year=1992 |isbn=4-582-53506-2}} * {{Cite book|author=光田重幸 |title=検索入門しだの図鑑 |publisher=保育社 |year=1986 |isbn=4-586-31011-1}}
== External Links == * {{NCBI|32163|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (NCBI) * {{EOL|487138|''Lepisorus thunbergianus''}} (Encyclopedia of Life) * [https://www1.ous.ac.jp/garden/hada/plantsdic/pteridophyta/polypodiaceae/nokisinobu/nokisinobu.htm 岡山理科大学 - ノキシノブ 植物図鑑]
